title: SQL Injection in Login via String Concatenation
description: User-supplied email is directly concatenated into a raw SQL query: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email}'`. An attacker can send a payload like `' OR '1'='1' --` to bypass authentication entirely. No parameterized queries or ORM bindings are used.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: The search criteria from query parameter `q` is concatenated directly into a raw SQL LIKE clause without escaping. This allows UNION-based injection to extract arbitrary data from the database, including user credentials.

title: Server-Side Template Injection via eval() in Username Field
description: If the username matches the pattern `#{...}`, the inner expression is passed to `eval()`. An attacker can set their username to `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id')}` to achieve remote code execution on the server.

title: NoSQL Injection via MongoDB $where Operator
description: The product ID from the URL path is concatenated directly into a MongoDB `$where` JavaScript expression: `db.reviewsCollection.find({ $where: 'this.product == ' + id })`. This allows JavaScript injection through the `$where` clause, enabling data exfiltration or query manipulation.

title: XXE Injection in XML File Upload
description: XML files are parsed with `{ noent: true }` which enables entity expansion. An attacker can upload an XML file containing an external entity declaration pointing to `/etc/passwd` or internal services, causing the server to read and return arbitrary file contents.

title: Code Injection via eval() in CAPTCHA Expression
description: CAPTCHA math expressions are evaluated with `eval()`. While the expression is server-generated, any code path that allows user influence over this value would enable arbitrary JavaScript execution. Using `eval()` in this context is a dangerous pattern.

title: Password Hashing with MD5
description: All user passwords are hashed using MD5 via `crypto.createHash('md5')`. MD5 is cryptographically broken — collisions are trivial to produce and the algorithm is fast enough that billions of guesses per second are possible on commodity hardware, making password databases trivially crackable.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key for JWT Signing
description: A 1024-bit RSA private key is hardcoded directly in the source file and used to sign all JWTs. Any attacker with read access to the source code can forge authentication tokens for any user, including administrators.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key
description: The HMAC secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is hardcoded in source. Any operation relying on this HMAC for integrity verification (e.g., coupon codes, tokens) can be forged by anyone who reads the repository.

title: Cryptographically Weak PRNG Used for CAPTCHA
description: `Math.random()` is used to generate CAPTCHA operands and operators. `Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure and is predictable if the seed or prior outputs are known, allowing automated CAPTCHA bypass.

title: Path Traversal via ZIP Extraction
description: ZIP archive entries are extracted using filenames taken directly from the archive: `fs.createWriteStream('uploads/complaints/' + fileName)`. The containment check `absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.'))` is bypassable via path components that resolve outside the expected directory, allowing arbitrary file writes.

title: Path Traversal in Log File Server
description: The log file endpoint accepts a `file` parameter and only checks for literal `/`. This check can be bypassed using URL-encoded traversal sequences, null bytes, or OS-specific tricks, potentially allowing reads of arbitrary files from the server.

title: Path Traversal in Quarantine File Server
description: Same pattern as logfileServer — a user-supplied filename is used in `path.resolve('ftp/quarantine/', file)` with only a forward-slash check as protection. Encoding tricks could allow reading files outside the quarantine directory.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference — Address Lookup
description: `UserId` is read from the request body (`req.body.UserId`) without verifying it matches the authenticated user's ID. An attacker can substitute any user's ID to read or manipulate another account's saved addresses.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference — Deluxe Membership & Wallet
description: Deluxe membership upgrades and wallet decrements use `req.body.UserId` without validating it belongs to the authenticated session. An attacker can upgrade or debit arbitrary user accounts by supplying a different `UserId` in the request body.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery in Profile Image Upload
description: A user-supplied URL in `req.body.imageUrl` is fetched server-side via `fetch(url)` with no domain allowlist or scheme restriction. An attacker can provide internal URLs (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/`) to probe cloud metadata endpoints or internal services.

title: Open Redirect via Substring Whitelist Bypass
description: `isRedirectAllowed()` uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` to check the destination URL. An attacker can craft a URL like `https://evil.com/?ref=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop` which passes the check but redirects to an attacker-controlled domain.

title: Sensitive Fields Exposed via Arbitrary Field Selection
description: The `/whoami` endpoint accepts a `?fields=` query parameter and returns any requested user fields, including `password` (MD5 hash) and `totpSecret`. An authenticated user can extract their own or, combined with IDOR, other users' credential data.

title: CORS Configured to Allow All Origins
description: `app.use(cors())` with no options accepts requests from any origin. Combined with the lack of CSRF protection, this allows malicious websites to make authenticated cross-origin requests on behalf of logged-in users.

title: Helmet XSS Filter Deliberately Disabled
description: The line `app.use(helmet.xssFilter())` is commented out, removing the `X-XSS-Protection` header. This eliminates the browser's reflected XSS heuristic filter, increasing risk from reflected XSS payloads.

title: JSONP Endpoint Enables Data Leakage
description: When a `?callback=` parameter is provided to the current-user endpoint, `res.jsonp()` wraps the full user object (including email and role) in the callback. This can be exploited via a `<script src>` tag on a malicious page to steal user profile data cross-origin.

title: Missing HttpOnly/Secure Flags on JWT Cookie
description: `res.cookie('token', updatedToken)` sets the authentication token cookie without `HttpOnly` or `Secure` flags. Without `HttpOnly`, the token is accessible to JavaScript, making it stealable via XSS; without `Secure`, it may be transmitted over HTTP.

title: Insecure YAML Deserialization in File Upload
description: YAML content from uploaded files is parsed with `js-yaml` version 3.x using the default `yaml.load()` (not `yaml.safeLoad()`). In older js-yaml versions, `yaml.load()` can instantiate arbitrary JavaScript objects, potentially leading to code execution via crafted YAML payloads.

title: Outdated express-jwt with Known Vulnerabilities
description: `express-jwt` version `0.1.3` is extremely old (circa 2013) and has multiple known security vulnerabilities including algorithm confusion attacks. A recent version should be used with the `algorithms` option explicitly specified.

title: Outdated sanitize-html Library
description: `sanitize-html` version `1.4.2` is significantly outdated and may contain XSS bypass vulnerabilities that have been patched in later versions. Outdated HTML sanitizers frequently have known bypass techniques discovered post-release.

title: js-yaml v3 with Unsafe load() Default
description: `js-yaml ^3.14.0` uses `yaml.load()` which in v3 can deserialize arbitrary JavaScript objects. The safe alternative `yaml.safeLoad()` should be used, or the library upgraded to v4+ where `load()` is safe by default.

title: Null Byte Sanitization Only Covers URL-Encoded Form
description: `cutOffPoisonNullByte()` only strips `%00` (URL-encoded null). Raw null bytes (`\x00`) or alternate encodings (`%2500`, double URL-encoding) may pass through undetected, potentially enabling null byte injection against path or string operations downstream.

title: Weak Random Secret in denyAll() Middleware
description: `denyAll()` uses `'' + Math.random()` as a JWT secret. Since `Math.random()` is not cryptographically random and has limited entropy, a determined attacker could potentially predict or brute-force the value to forge tokens accepted by this middleware.

title: Client IP Address Leaked in Error Message
description: The error message `'Blocked illegal activity by ' + req.socket.remoteAddress` includes the client IP, which is then passed to the error handler and may be surfaced in HTTP responses. This leaks network topology information and real IPs when behind proxies.

title: SSRF Detection Marker Does Not Block Requests
description: The code detects if the SSRF challenge URL pattern is used and sets `req.app.locals.abused_ssrf_bug = true`, but does not block the request. This is intentional for the challenge but confirms SSRF is fully exploitable against any internal URL.
